content.assessment: This proposal has a plausible actor (the newly committed Minister has already signaled intent to place admissions on the agenda), and admissions-mechanism redesign is a narrower, more procedurally contained lever than full de-tracking, improving pilotability and failure containment relative to T2/T6. However, coverage gaps (church/private schools outside central authority) and divided union positions constitute concrete missing coordination capabilities.
opportunities: Ministerial commitment to social consultation (F-live-implementation_planning-05) could be leveraged as a formal sequencing and feedback step before rollout
Could be combined with the accountability-review mechanism (National Pedagogue Chamber proposal) to add a monitoring layer absent from the base design
strengths: A credible, currently-in-office actor has already stated intent to pursue this reform, improving political feasibility of initiating sequencing (F-live-implementation_planning-05)
Narrower scope (admissions mechanism only, track structure preserved) reduces the coordination burden relative to full structural reform, aiding containment
threats: Underlying rationale for admissions reform is itself contested — the minister's claim that gains are family-background-driven is disputed moderate-strength evidence, meaning the diagnosis animating the reform is not settled (F-live-implementation_planning-02, F-live-implementation_planning-05)
Historical precedent of desegregation measures 'stagnating mid-decade' raises reversal or fatigue risk absent a specified adaptation mechanism
weaknesses: Church-maintained and private schools sit outside the central authority responsible for implementing admissions changes, creating a structural coverage gap that undermines uniform delivery
Divided union positions on the underlying goal (phase-out vs. broader segregation focus) suggest missing coordination consensus needed for implementation support
